payload.answer: Knowledge graphs organize data as interconnected entities and relationships, enabling flexible querying and reasoning across diverse data sources. They are built from nodes (entities) and edges (relationships), often using a schema or ontology to define types and properties. Common tasks include integrating data from multiple systems, identifying connected information (entity resolution), and performing inferences. If you’d like, I can provide a beginner guide to components, data models (RDF/property graphs), popular query languages (SPARQL, Cypher), and hands-on tutorials.

memory_cards.statement: A knowledge graph is a structured representation of real-world entities, their attributes, and the relationships between them, typically modeled as a graph of nodes (entities) connected by edges (relationships).
Key concepts include nodes (entities), edges (relationships), properties (attributes), schemas (ontologies), and triples (subject–predicate–object).
Knowledge graphs enable intelligent querying, data integration, entity resolution, and inference across heterogeneous data sources.
